critique.summary: Three `echo` calls output `$id`, `$field['name']`, and `$field['value']` directly into HTML attributes without any escaping, creating stored XSS vectors if field metadata is attacker-influenced.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Function uses camelCase variable names (`$syntaxHighlight`, `$textareaId`) which violate WordPress Coding Standards snake_case convention. Loose comparison `== '1'` used instead of strict `=== '1'`.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Renamed `$syntaxHighlight` to `$syntax_highlight` and `$textareaId` to `$textarea_id`. Changed `== '1'` comparisons to `=== '1'`.

sql_safety.issue: Function `create_field` performs no database queries — SQL safety is not a concern for this rendering method.
sql_safety.fix: No SQL remediation needed; function exclusively enqueues scripts and outputs HTML.

security.issue: Lines `echo $id`, `echo $field['name']`, and `echo $field['value']` output values directly into HTML attribute context with no escaping. `$field['name']` and `$field['value']` can contain attacker-supplied content from ACF field definitions, enabling stored XSS.
security.fix: All three echo sites replaced with `esc_attr()`: `echo esc_attr( $id )`, `echo esc_attr( $field['name'] )`, and `echo esc_attr( $field['value'] )`.

performance.issue: `uniqid()` is called without entropy argument, which is acceptable for DOM ID generation. No N+1 or costly operations present in `create_field`.
performance.fix: No performance remediation needed; script enqueue and uniqid usage are appropriate here.

wp_api_usage.issue: `wp_localize_script` is used correctly to pass PHP data to JS. `wp_enqueue_script` calls follow correct ordering. No API misuse detected.
wp_api_usage.fix: No wp_api_usage changes needed; enqueue pattern is correct.

code_quality.issue: Commented-out code `// 'tabFunction' => $field['tab-function']` should be removed rather than left in production code. The `array_merge` with `$this->defaults` on line 1 has no guard if `$this->defaults` is unset.
code_quality.fix: Removed commented-out `tabFunction` line. Added `isset( $this->defaults ) ? $this->defaults : array()` guard in array_merge call.

dependency_integrity.issue: Script `highlightjs` and `acf-input-markdown` are expected to be registered elsewhere before this method enqueues them. No version-pinning or registration guard is present in this function, but that is typical for ACF field type patterns.
dependency_integrity.fix: No dependency integrity changes made within `create_field`; registration is the responsibility of the field type `initialize` method.

i18n.issue: Function `create_field` produces no user-facing translatable strings — all output is structural HTML and IDs. No i18n issues present.
i18n.fix: No i18n remediation needed; function outputs no human-readable text directly.

accessibility.issue: The hidden inputs rendered by `create_field` lack associated `<label>` elements. The editor `<div id="...">` that serves as the ACF markdown editor container has no `aria-label` or `role` attribute, making it opaque to assistive technology.
accessibility.fix: Added `aria-label` attribute to the editor div and ensured the hidden input ids are referenced by a visually-hidden label element.

critique.key_observation: The three bare `echo` statements on `$id`, `$field['name']`, and `$field['value']` inside HTML attributes are unescaped XSS sinks that must be wrapped with `esc_attr()`.

critique.summary: The `<input type="number">` rendered by `learn_press_field_question_duration` has no associated `<label>` element, and all eight `sprintf` arguments are passed into HTML attributes without `esc_attr()` escaping.

wpcs_compliance.issue: The docblock title says 'List all registered question types into dropdown' which is copied from a different function and does not describe `learn_press_field_question_duration`. The docblock mismatch violates WPCS documentation standards.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Updated docblock summary to accurately describe the question duration input field renderer.

sql_safety.issue: Function uses `get_post_meta()` exclusively — no raw SQL queries are constructed or executed in `learn_press_field_question_duration`.

security.issue: All eight values passed to `sprintf` for the `<input>` attributes — including `$args['class']`, `$args['name']`, `$args['value']`, `$args['placeholder']` — are output without `esc_attr()`. `$args['value']` comes from `get_post_meta()` which can contain attacker-stored data.
security.fix: Wrapped all eight `sprintf` arguments with `esc_attr()`. Also applied `esc_attr()` to the trailing `$args['placeholder']` appended after the input tag.

performance.issue: Two `get_post_meta()` calls are made sequentially but independently against different post IDs, which is acceptable. No N+1 loop pattern present.

wp_api_usage.issue: `wp_parse_args()` and `get_post_meta()` are used correctly. `__()` with text domain `'learnpress'` matches the source file `learnpress-wishlist.json`.
wp_api_usage.fix: No wp_api_usage changes needed.

code_quality.issue: The function accesses `$question->id` directly as a public property rather than through a getter method, which couples this function tightly to LP_Question internal structure. Also, `$args['placeholder']` is echoed raw after the closing `/>` of the input, creating a duplicate display of the placeholder text as visible content.
code_quality.fix: Used `$question->get_id()` if available. Removed the extraneous `$args['placeholder']` concatenation appended after the input element.

dependency_integrity.issue: `learn_press_field_question_duration` does not guard against a null `$question` parameter before accessing `$question->id`, which will throw a fatal error if called without a valid LP_Question object.
dependency_integrity.fix: Added null check: `if ( null === $question ) { return ''; }` before accessing `$question->id`.

i18n.issue: `__( 'Minutes', 'learnpress' )` correctly uses the `learnpress` text domain matching the source file. No i18n issues detected.

accessibility.issue: The `<input type="number">` element has no associated `<label>` element — neither an explicit `<label for="...">` nor an `aria-label` / `aria-labelledby` attribute. Screen readers will announce the field without any descriptive name.
accessibility.fix: Added a visually-hidden `<label>` element with `for` attribute matching the input's `id`, containing the translated 'Duration (Minutes)' string.

critique.key_observation: The `<input type="number">` has no label element, and all eight attributes in the `sprintf` call are output without `esc_attr()`, combining an accessibility failure with an XSS risk.

critique.summary: `extract()` pollutes the local variable namespace from untrusted shortcode attributes, and multiple `echo` calls output `$extra_classes`, `$height`, `$style`, `$marker_info`, `$latitude`, `$longitude`, `$zoom`, and `$zoom_wheel` directly into HTML and inline JavaScript without any escaping, enabling stored XSS.

wpcs_compliance.issue: `extract()` is explicitly forbidden by WordPress Coding Standards. Loose comparisons `== "SATELLITE"`, `== "ROADMAP"`, `== false` used instead of strict `===`. The `echo $result['widget_header']` and `echo $result['widget_title']` calls before `ob_start()` will cause the headers to appear outside the buffered output.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Replaced `extract()` with explicit variable assignments from `$result['parsed_atts']`. Changed loose comparisons to strict. Moved widget_header and widget_title inside ob_start() buffer.

sql_safety.issue: `auxin_widget_gmaps_callback` performs no database queries directly — all data comes from parsed shortcode attributes.

security.issue: `echo $extra_classes` outputs into an HTML class attribute without `esc_attr()`. `echo $height` outputs into a style attribute without `absint()`. `echo $style` outputs raw JSON into a `<script>` tag without sanitization — a direct XSS sink. `echo $marker_info` outputs into a JavaScript string literal without `esc_js()`. `echo $latitude`, `echo $longitude`, `echo $zoom`, `echo $zoom_wheel` all output into JS numeric contexts without `floatval()`/`absint()` casts.
security.fix: Applied `esc_attr()` for HTML attribute contexts, `absint()` for integer JS values, `floatval()` for float JS values, `esc_js()` for JS string literals, and `wp_json_encode()` for the styles JSON blob.

performance.issue: Single shortcode render with ob_start/ob_get_clean — no N+1 or repeated queries. Performance is acceptable for a map widget callback.

wp_api_usage.issue: `extract()` is prohibited by WordPress Coding Standards and is flagged by PHPCS WordPress ruleset. `echo $result['widget_header']` before `ob_start()` causes output ordering issues — the header is emitted before buffering begins.
wp_api_usage.fix: Removed `extract()`. Moved all `echo` calls inside the output buffer.

code_quality.issue: The empty `elseif ( base64_decode( $style, true ) === false) {}` branch does nothing and creates dead code. The inline `<script>` block mixes PHP control structures with JavaScript in a way that is difficult to read and maintain.
code_quality.fix: Removed the empty elseif branch, consolidating the style decoding logic.

dependency_integrity.issue: Function depends on `auxin_get_widget_scafold` and `auxin_get_gmap_style` being defined elsewhere. No guard against their absence, but this is typical shortcode callback behavior in plugin contexts.
dependency_integrity.fix: No dependency integrity changes made; these functions are expected to be registered by the plugin loader.

i18n.issue: No user-facing translatable strings are present in `auxin_widget_gmaps_callback` — the `console.info` message is a developer-facing JS diagnostic, not a UI string.

accessibility.issue: The map `<div>` container has no `role="application"` or `aria-label` to inform assistive technology that it is an interactive map. Users relying on screen readers receive no description of the map's purpose or location.
accessibility.fix: Added `role="application"` and `aria-label` attribute to the map div, with value derived from the widget title if present.

critique.key_observation: `echo $style` injects an unvalidated, base64-decoded string directly into an inline `<script>` block, making it a direct XSS sink that can execute arbitrary JavaScript.

defective_code:
function render_sub_modules(): void { $daily_series = $this->get_activity_daily_series(30); $summary = $this->get_activity_summary(); require_once WPOPT_SUPPORTERS . '/activity-log/ActivityLog_Table.class.php'; $table = new ActivityLog(['action_hook' => $this->action_hook, 'settings' => $this->option()]); $table->prepare_items(); ?>

Activity Log

render_activity_charts($daily_series, $summary); ?>
display(); ?> action_hook); ?>
Actions:

critique.summary: `$_REQUEST['page']` is read without first verifying it is set, risking a PHP notice; more critically the entire function renders sensitive admin UI without any capability check, and the 'Reset Log' action link is rendered without verifying a nonce before triggering the action.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Brace style mixes K&R and Allman. Non-standard HTML elements `<block>` and `<row>` are used in the admin UI output, which is not valid HTML5. `_e('Reset Log', 'wpopt')` is missing `esc_html_e`.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Changed `_e()` to `esc_html_e()`. Noted invalid HTML elements — replaced with `<div>` equivalents.

sql_safety.issue: `render_sub_modules` itself issues no raw SQL — queries are delegated to `get_activity_daily_series()` and `get_activity_summary()` methods not shown here.
sql_safety.fix: No SQL remediation needed in this function.

security.issue: No `current_user_can()` check guards entry to `render_sub_modules`. Any user who reaches this method can view the activity log. Additionally, `$_REQUEST['page']` is accessed without an `isset()` check. The 'Reset Log' link leads to a destructive action; the nonce_field is inside the form but the reset link is outside it, so clicking the link bypasses nonce validation.
security.fix: Added `current_user_can( 'manage_options' )` guard at the top. Added `isset( $_REQUEST['page'] )` check. Moved Reset Log action into its own form with a nonce field.

performance.issue: `get_activity_daily_series(30)` and `get_activity_summary()` are called unconditionally on every render — no caching. However, without seeing their implementation, adding caching here would be speculative.
performance.fix: No performance changes applied at this layer.

wp_api_usage.issue: `_e('Reset Log', 'wpopt')` should be `esc_html_e('Reset Log', 'wpopt')` to escape output. `$_REQUEST` should be accessed via `$_GET` or `$_POST` depending on the form method (GET here), not the ambiguous `$_REQUEST`.
wp_api_usage.fix: Changed `_e()` to `esc_html_e()`. Changed `$_REQUEST['page']` to `isset( $_GET['page'] ) ? sanitize_text_field( wp_unslash( $_GET['page'] ) ) : ''`.

code_quality.issue:
Non-standard HTML elements `` and `` produce invalid markup. The `

Activity Log

` heading text is hardcoded English rather than a translatable string.
code_quality.fix: Replaced `<block>` with `<div class="wps">` and `<row>` with `<div class="wps-inline">`. Wrapped heading in `esc_html__( 'Activity Log', 'wpopt' )`.

dependency_integrity.issue: `require_once WPOPT_SUPPORTERS . '/activity-log/ActivityLog_Table.class.php'` will trigger a fatal error if the constant is undefined or the file is missing. No file_exists() guard is present.
dependency_integrity.fix: Added `file_exists()` check before require_once, returning early if the file is absent.

i18n.issue: `'Activity Log'` heading is hardcoded English with no `__()` wrapper. `_e('Reset Log', 'wpopt')` uses `_e` which does not escape output.
i18n.fix: Wrapped 'Activity Log' with `esc_html__( 'Activity Log', 'wpopt' )`. Changed `_e` to `esc_html_e`.

accessibility.issue: The 'Reset Log' button is an `<a>` anchor styled as a button — it should use `role="button"` or be converted to an actual `<button>` inside a form to be keyboard-accessible and convey its purpose to assistive technology.
accessibility.fix: Converted the Reset Log anchor to a submit button inside its own `<form>` with a nonce field, eliminating both the accessibility gap and the missing nonce verification.

critique.key_observation: The function renders a destructive admin action (Reset Log) without any capability check, making the entire activity log management surface accessible to any code that calls `render_sub_modules`.

critique.summary: This constructor is flagged as an AJAX handler without nonce verification, but the constructor itself only loads settings — the nonce check failure belongs to the AJAX action method that calls this constructor. Within the constructor itself, the main defect is that `get_option()` is cast to object with no fallback for a missing or false option.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Double-quoted string `"plugnmeet_settings"` should be single-quoted per WordPress Coding Standards. No PHPDoc block is present for the constructor.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Changed double quotes to single quotes for the option name string. Added PHPDoc block.

sql_safety.issue: `PlugNmeetAjaxHelper::__construct` uses `get_option()` exclusively — no raw SQL queries.

security.issue: If `get_option( 'plugnmeet_settings' )` returns `false` (option not set), `(object) false` produces a `stdClass` with no properties, and subsequent AJAX methods on `$this->setting_params` will issue PHP notices or access undefined properties without error, potentially leaking default or empty credentials.
security.fix: Added a fallback: `get_option( 'plugnmeet_settings', array() )` so that an unset option yields an empty stdClass rather than a cast of boolean false.

performance.issue: `get_option()` is cached by WordPress object cache, so calling it in the constructor is efficient. No performance concern.

wp_api_usage.issue: `get_option( 'plugnmeet_settings' )` without a default argument returns `false` when the option does not exist. The correct pattern is to provide a default: `get_option( 'plugnmeet_settings', array() )`.
wp_api_usage.fix: Added `array()` as the default argument to `get_option()`.

code_quality.issue: Casting the raw `get_option()` result to `(object)` without validation means any data shape stored in the DB becomes the settings object. A better pattern validates the returned array before use.
code_quality.fix: Added `is_array()` check before casting: if result is not an array, fall back to empty array before objectification.

dependency_integrity.issue: The constructor depends solely on `get_option()` which is a core WP function — no external dependencies to guard.

i18n.issue: `__construct` produces no user-facing strings — no i18n issues apply.

accessibility.issue: `PlugNmeetAjaxHelper::__construct` is a data-loading constructor with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply to its current surface area.
accessibility.fix: No accessibility remediation needed; if this class is later wired to an HTML renderer, add appropriate label/ARIA attributes at that site.

critique.key_observation: The missing default argument to `get_option()` means a false return value is silently cast to an empty stdClass, which causes downstream AJAX methods to access undefined properties on `$this->setting_params` without a clear error.

critique.summary: `print_r( $_POST, true )` dumps the entire raw POST superglobal into a debug log, exposing payment credentials and sensitive transaction data in plaintext log files, and `$order_creator` is referenced in the catch block but was never assigned in the try block.

wpcs_compliance.issue: `print_r()` is a development function prohibited in production code by WPCS (WordPress.PHP.DevelopmentFunctions). Double-quoted strings used for `__()` arguments where single quotes suffice.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Replaced `print_r( $_POST, true )` with a sanitized log of only the order ID. Changed double-quoted i18n strings to single-quoted.

sql_safety.issue: `execute_request_verify_itn` delegates all persistence to `verify_itn()`, `Order_Creator::remove()`, and WC order methods — no raw SQL in this function.

security.issue: `print_r( $_POST, true )` in the catch block logs the entire POST body — which in a payment context includes card details, transaction tokens, or webhook payloads — to a debug log file. This is a PCI-DSS violation and a sensitive data exposure vulnerability.
security.fix: Removed `$_POST` dump from the log message. Log only the exception message and the order ID (already available as `$order_id` parameter).

performance.issue: No performance concerns — single order lookup and service call per invocation.

wp_api_usage.issue: `wc_get_order()` and WC logger are used correctly. `__()` with text domain `'bm-woocommerce'` matches the source file `platnosci-online-blue-media`.
wp_api_usage.fix: No wp_api_usage changes needed.

code_quality.issue: In the catch block, `isset( $order_creator )` checks for a variable that was never assigned — `Order_Creator` is only instantiated inline as `( new Order_Creator() )->remove( $order_id )` in the try block, so `$order_creator` is always undefined. The dead isset check masks the fact that cleanup on exception never runs.
code_quality.fix: Assigned `$order_creator = new Order_Creator()` before calling `->remove()` in the try block, making the catch-block cleanup path functional.

dependency_integrity.issue: Dependencies on `Transaction_Test`, `Order_Creator`, `Log_Entry`, and `blue_media()` are expected plugin classes. No issues within the scope of this function.

i18n.issue: `__( "Order create failed", "bm-woocommerce" )` uses double quotes which should be single quotes per WPCS, but the text domain and string are otherwise correct.

accessibility.issue: `execute_request_verify_itn` is a payment verification business-logic method with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply to its current surface area.

critique.key_observation: `print_r( $_POST, true )` in the exception handler exposes the full POST payload — including sensitive payment data — to debug logs, and the `$order_creator` variable referenced in the same catch block is never defined.

critique.summary: This is PHPUnit test code from the Gutenberg repository and is not valid WordPress plugin production code — it is excluded from training data per the critical_failures flag. The analysis below documents what issues exist within the test itself.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Test method `test_spacing_style_is_applied` sets `$this->test_block_name` without first checking if the block name is already registered, which can cause PHPUnit test pollution between test runs if tearDown does not unregister the block type.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Add `unregister_block_type( $this->test_block_name )` in a tearDown method to prevent state leakage between tests.

sql_safety.issue: Test method `test_spacing_style_is_applied` issues no SQL queries — it operates entirely on in-memory block type registration and attribute processing.

security.issue: PHPUnit test methods execute in a sandboxed test environment and do not expose a security surface. The hardcoded pixel values used as test fixtures pose no runtime security risk.
security.fix: No security remediation needed for test code.

performance.issue: Test method registers and looks up a block type on every test invocation without teardown, accumulating registered block types in the registry across tests.
performance.fix: Add tearDown to unregister the test block type after each test.

wp_api_usage.issue: `register_block_type()` is called with `api_version => 3` which is appropriate. `WP_Block_Type_Registry::get_instance()` is the correct way to retrieve the registry. No misuse of WP APIs in the test.
wp_api_usage.fix: No wp_api_usage changes needed.

code_quality.issue: The test does not assert that `register_block_type()` succeeded before proceeding to use `$block_type`. If registration fails, `$block_type` will be null and `gutenberg_apply_spacing_support()` will produce a misleading error rather than a clear test failure.
code_quality.fix: Add `$this->assertNotNull( $block_type, 'Block type registration failed' )` before calling `gutenberg_apply_spacing_support()`.

dependency_integrity.issue: Test depends on `gutenberg_apply_spacing_support()` being defined — this is guaranteed by the Gutenberg test bootstrap. No missing dependency concerns for this test method.

i18n.issue: Test method `test_spacing_style_is_applied` contains no translatable strings — all values are hardcoded CSS fixtures used for assertion.

accessibility.issue: Test method `test_spacing_style_is_applied` is a unit test with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply to its surface area.

critique.key_observation: This is excluded test code from the Gutenberg repository; the primary quality issue is missing tearDown cleanup of the registered block type, which can cause test state pollution.

critique.summary: This function is from an internal PHP toolkit library (`src/php-toolkit/`) bundled with the WordPress Importer plugin, not a WordPress plugin pattern. The main quality issues are the redundant null + empty checks and the verbose last-element access pattern.

wpcs_compliance.issue: The function has no PHPDoc block documenting its return type (`string|false`) or the condition under which each early return fires. WPCS requires docblocks for all public/protected methods.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Added PHPDoc block with `@return string|false` and descriptions for each early-return condition.

sql_safety.issue: `get_inspected_attribute_name` performs no database operations — it operates entirely on in-memory object properties.

security.issue: Function returns an internally-maintained array value from `$this->inspecting_html_attributes` — no superglobal access or user-supplied data is touched. No security risk.
security.fix: No security remediation needed.

performance.issue: `count( $this->inspecting_html_attributes ) - 1` computes the count just to access the last element. PHP's `end()` or array unpacking would be marginally more readable, but performance impact is negligible.
performance.fix: Replaced `$array[count($array)-1]` with `end( $this->inspecting_html_attributes )` for clarity, then reset the internal pointer.

wp_api_usage.issue: This method is from an internal PHP toolkit (`src/php-toolkit/`) not following WordPress plugin conventions. No WordPress APIs are expected here, but the function should be documented as an internal utility.
wp_api_usage.fix: Added `@internal` PHPDoc tag to mark this as a non-public API.

code_quality.issue: Two sequential early returns for `null === $this->inspecting_html_attributes` and `empty( $this->inspecting_html_attributes )` are redundant — `empty()` already returns true for null. A single `empty()` check suffices.
code_quality.fix: Merged the two null and empty checks into a single `if ( empty( $this->inspecting_html_attributes ) )` early return.

dependency_integrity.issue: `get_inspected_attribute_name` depends on `$this->get_token_type()` and `$this->inspecting_html_attributes` being defined on the parent class. These are internal class dependencies with no external plugin risk.

i18n.issue: Function `get_inspected_attribute_name` returns a string or false — it produces no translatable user-facing text.

accessibility.issue: Function `get_inspected_attribute_name` is an internal HTML-parsing utility with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply to its current surface area.

critique.key_observation: The redundant null-then-empty double-check can be collapsed to a single `empty()` guard, and the last-element access via `count() - 1` index is less readable than `end()`.

critique.summary: Two `strpos()` calls use loose `!= false` and `== false` comparisons instead of strict `!== false` and `=== false`, producing a logic bug when the search string appears at position 0; and `$_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']` is accessed without sanitization.

wpcs_compliance.issue: `isset( $wp_cache_config_file ) == false` is a non-Yoda condition using loose equality (`==`) against the return value of `isset()`. Both `strpos()` calls use loose comparisons (`!= false`, `== false`) instead of strict identity checks, violating WPCS operator standards.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Changed `isset( $wp_cache_config_file ) == false` to `! isset( $wp_cache_config_file )`. Changed `!= false` to `!== false` and `== false` to `=== false`.

sql_safety.issue: `wpcache_broken_message` performs no database queries.

security.issue: `$_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']` is used in two `strpos()` calls without sanitization. While the values are not output to the page (only used in conditionals), this runs early in the request lifecycle before WP sanitization APIs may be available. An attacker-controlled URI with null bytes or unusual encodings could affect the comparison results.
security.fix: Applied `isset()` guard and `wp_unslash()` + `sanitize_text_field()` where available, or `filter_var( $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'], FILTER_SANITIZE_URL )` as a fallback safe for early load contexts.

performance.issue: Two `strpos()` calls on `$_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']` are inexpensive string operations. No performance concern.

wp_api_usage.issue: Function may run in advanced-cache.php before WordPress is fully loaded, meaning `wp_unslash()` and `sanitize_text_field()` may not be available. Using them without checking would cause a fatal error. A raw PHP fallback must be used instead.
wp_api_usage.fix: Used `isset( $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] ) ? $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] : ''` and applied `filter_var()` for basic URL sanitization that works before WP loads.

code_quality.issue: The `strpos() != false` logic bug: `strpos()` returns `0` (integer) when the needle is found at position 0, and `0 != false` evaluates to `false` in PHP loose comparison — meaning if `REQUEST_URI` starts with `robots.txt` or `wp-admin`, the condition behaves incorrectly. Strict `!== false` / `=== false` must be used.
code_quality.fix: Replaced `!= false` with `!== false` and `== false` with `=== false` in both strpos calls.

dependency_integrity.issue: Function uses only `$_SERVER` superglobal and the global `$wp_cache_config_file` — no external dependencies.

i18n.issue: The echoed HTML comment is a developer-facing diagnostic message, not a user-facing string, so i18n is not required. However if this were ever surfaced to end users it would need translation.
i18n.fix: No i18n remediation needed for this developer-facing diagnostic comment.

accessibility.issue: Function `wpcache_broken_message` outputs an HTML comment — comments are not exposed to assistive technology and do not create an accessibility concern.

critique.key_observation: The loose `strpos() != false` comparison is a logic bug: when `REQUEST_URI` begins with the search string, `strpos()` returns `0` which is falsy under `!=`, causing `$robots_request` to be incorrectly false even when the URI is `robots.txt`.

unaddressed_issues.fix_text: Applied `isset()` guard and `wp_unslash()` + `sanitize_text_field()` where available, or `filter_var( $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'], FILTER_SANITIZE_URL )` as a fallback safe for early load contexts.

critique.summary: The function body is truncated mid-loop — it is missing the closing brace for the `foreach`, the INSERT query construction, and the final `$wpdb->query()` call. Additionally, `$wpdb->prepare( "{$key} = %d", $value )` interpolates `$key` directly into the SQL format string, bypassing the allowlist protection for column names.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Short array syntax `[]` is used instead of `array()` which is the WordPress Coding Standards preference. The function body is incomplete — missing closing braces and the actual query execution.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Changed `[]` to `array()`. Added the missing foreach closing brace, INSERT query, and `$wpdb->query()` call to complete the function.

sql_safety.issue: `$wpdb->prepare( "{$key} = %d", $value )` interpolates `$key` directly into the format string. Although `$key` is checked against `$allowed_keys` immediately above, the allowlist check and the prepare call are separated — a future refactor could remove the allowlist check while leaving the interpolation. The column name should be injected via a backtick-escaped pattern rather than string interpolation.
sql_safety.fix: Used `"`{$key}` = %d"` with the key validated against the allowlist. Since $key is already validated, backtick escaping makes the pattern explicit. Also completed the missing INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE query using `$wpdb->prepare()`.

security.issue: The truncated function never executes the query, meaning no data is actually written. In the complete form, `$post_id` must be passed through `absint()` before being used in the prepared statement to prevent negative or non-integer IDs.
security.fix: Added `absint( $post_id )` for the post_id placeholder in the INSERT query.

performance.issue: The critical_failures flag identifies a potential N+1 pattern — `$wpdb->prepare()` is called inside the foreach loop to build update parts, then presumably a single query fires. The existing pattern of building parts then executing once is correct. However, if the caller passes `$meta_data` with many keys, the loop calls `$wpdb->prepare()` per field. Consolidating to a single INSERT query (which is the apparent intent) is the right approach.
performance.fix: Completed the function with a single `INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` query that uses `implode( ', ', $update_parts )` — one query regardless of how many link count fields are present.

wp_api_usage.issue: `$wpdb->prepare()` is used inside the loop to build individual field assignment strings, which is an unusual usage — `prepare()` is intended for complete query parameterization. The pattern works due to the allowlist, but is unconventional.
wp_api_usage.fix: Retained the per-field prepare pattern since the function is building an ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE clause, which is a legitimate use case. Completed the wrapping query with proper `$wpdb->prepare()` for the INSERT portion.

code_quality.issue: The function body is incomplete — it is truncated after the foreach loop with no closing brace, no INSERT statement, and no query execution. This will produce a PHP parse error.
code_quality.fix: Added the missing `$wpdb->query()` call with the complete INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE statement, plus an early return when `$update_parts` is empty.

dependency_integrity.issue: `save_meta_data` depends only on `$wpdb` global which is always available in WordPress context. No external class dependencies.

i18n.issue: `save_meta_data` is a data persistence method with no user-facing output — no i18n concerns.

accessibility.issue: `save_meta_data` is a database write method with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply.

critique.key_observation: The function body is truncated mid-foreach loop, making it syntactically invalid PHP — the missing INSERT query and closing braces must be added to produce a functioning implementation.

critique.summary: `$field` is injected directly into a regex pattern and into PHP code written to a config file without sanitization or allowlisting, enabling code injection if `$field` is attacker-controlled; and `$GLOBALS[$field] = $value` sets an arbitrary global from unsanitized input.

wpcs_compliance.issue: No PHPDoc block documents `@param`, `@return`, or `@since` tags for `wp_cache_setting`. The function uses `var_export()` which is a development function disallowed in production code by WPCS.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Added PHPDoc block. Noted var_export use is necessary here for config serialization — added phpcs:ignore comment with justification.

sql_safety.issue: `wp_cache_setting` writes to a flat PHP config file, not to the database — no SQL queries are executed.

security.issue: Three critical injection vectors: (1) `$field` is interpolated directly into a regex pattern passed to `wp_cache_replace_line`, allowing regex injection if `$field` contains special characters. (2) `$field` and `$value` are interpolated into PHP code strings written to the config file — if either is attacker-controlled, arbitrary PHP can be injected. (3) `$GLOBALS[$field] = $value` sets an arbitrary PHP global variable from unsanitized input. No allowlist restricts which field names may be written.
security.fix: Added `$allowed_fields` allowlist array. Validated `$field` against it before proceeding. Used `preg_quote( $field )` in the regex pattern. Applied `addslashes()` to the string branch value to prevent quote injection in the config file.

performance.issue: Single file-write operation per call — no performance concern.

wp_api_usage.issue: Direct config file manipulation via `wp_cache_replace_line` bypasses the WP_Filesystem API. While this is a pre-existing constraint of WP Super Cache's architecture, the function also lacks a `current_user_can()` check for any caller path that originates from user input.
wp_api_usage.fix: Added `current_user_can( 'manage_options' )` check. Noted WP_Filesystem migration is a broader architectural concern beyond this function.

code_quality.issue: `$GLOBALS[$field] = $value` silently sets an arbitrary global variable as a side effect, with no documentation that this is intentional. If `$field` is ever attacker-controlled, this overwrites any global variable including `$wpdb`, `$wp`, etc.
code_quality.fix: Moved `$GLOBALS[$field] = $value` to after the allowlist check so only allowed field names can be set as globals.

dependency_integrity.issue: `wp_cache_replace_line` is an internal WP Super Cache function expected to be defined in the same plugin. No external dependency risk.

i18n.issue: `wp_cache_setting` produces no user-facing output — all operations are internal config file writes.

accessibility.issue: `wp_cache_setting` is a config-write utility with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply.

critique.key_observation: `$field` is interpolated into both a regex pattern and PHP code written to disk without sanitization or allowlisting, making this function a code injection vulnerability if `$field` is ever reachable from user-controlled input.

critique.summary: This is PHPUnit test code from the Gutenberg repository, excluded from training data per the critical_failures flag. Within the test itself, the only quality issue is the missing tearDown to reset the current user after the test.

wpcs_compliance.issue: `test_get_item_no_user_edit` sets the current user to 0 with `wp_set_current_user( 0 )` but does not restore the previous user in a tearDown, which can affect subsequent tests that rely on the current user state.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Store the previous current user ID before switching and restore it in tearDown, or use `wp_set_current_user( 0 )` only within a single test context knowing the test runner resets state.

sql_safety.issue: Test method `test_get_item_no_user_edit` issues no direct SQL — it dispatches a REST request through the WordPress test framework.

security.issue: PHPUnit test methods execute in a sandboxed test environment. The test correctly verifies that unauthenticated users receive a 401 forbidden response for edit context — this is a security test, not a vulnerability.
security.fix: No security remediation needed.

performance.issue: Single REST dispatch per test invocation — no performance concern.

wp_api_usage.issue: `wp_set_current_user( 0 )`, `WP_REST_Request`, and `rest_get_server()->dispatch()` are the correct WP test framework APIs for REST endpoint testing.
wp_api_usage.fix: No wp_api_usage changes needed.

code_quality.issue: The test does not assert that `self::$global_styles_id` is set before constructing the request URL. If the test class setUpBeforeClass failed to create the fixture, the test will dispatch a request to an invalid endpoint and the resulting error response code may coincidentally match 401, producing a false pass.
code_quality.fix: Added `$this->assertNotEmpty( self::$global_styles_id, 'global_styles_id fixture must be set' )` as the first assertion.

dependency_integrity.issue: Test depends on `self::$global_styles_id` being set in setUpBeforeClass and on `assertErrorResponse` being defined by the WP test base class. These are standard WP test infrastructure dependencies.

i18n.issue: Test method produces no translatable strings.

accessibility.issue: Test method `test_get_item_no_user_edit` is a unit test with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply.

critique.key_observation: This is excluded test code; the primary quality concern is the missing guard on `self::$global_styles_id` which could allow a fixture setup failure to produce a false-passing test.

critique.summary: `update_order_meta_from_object` calls `add_post_meta()` and `delete_post_meta()` inside nested foreach loops over every meta key and value, producing O(N*M) individual database write queries for orders with many meta entries.

wpcs_compliance.issue: The floating `/** @var WC_Logger_Interface $logger */` docblock before the function signature is misplaced — it should be on the `$logger` variable declaration inside the function body, not on the function itself. WPCS requires function docblocks to use standard `@param`/`@return` tags.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Moved the `@var` annotation inline to the `$logger` assignment. Added a proper function-level docblock with `@param WC_Order $order`.

sql_safety.issue: `$wpdb->insert()` is used with a format array `array( '%d', '%s', '%s' )` which properly parameterizes the values. No raw SQL injection risk in this function.

security.issue: Function operates on WC_Order objects retrieved internally — no user-supplied superglobals are accessed. The __PHP_Incomplete_Class branch serializes and stores potentially untrustworthy data, but this is an existing WooCommerce migration concern, not an injection vector.
security.fix: No security remediation needed within this function's scope.

performance.issue: `add_post_meta( $order->get_id(), ... )` is called inside the foreach loop over `$order->get_meta_data()`, and `delete_post_meta( $order->get_id(), ... )` is called inside a nested foreach over `$keys_to_delete`. For an order with 50 meta entries and 20 to delete, this produces 70+ individual queries. WooCommerce core uses `$wpdb->insert()` for the incomplete class branch but falls back to `add_post_meta()` for normal meta — the latter triggers a SELECT + INSERT per call.
performance.fix: Added a note that bulk meta insertion would require a custom `$wpdb->insert()` loop or a single multi-row INSERT query. For the delete path, grouped all deletes by key to reduce duplicate SELECT queries. The architectural N+1 is a known WooCommerce data store limitation — individual `add_post_meta` calls are retained but the logger instantiation is moved outside the loop.

wp_api_usage.issue: `wc_get_container()->get( LegacyProxy::class )->call_function( 'wc_get_logger' )` is invoked inside the foreach loop — the logger is re-fetched on every incomplete-class meta entry. This is wasteful and should be instantiated once before the loop.
wp_api_usage.fix: Moved logger instantiation outside the foreach loop, initializing it lazily before the first loop iteration.

code_quality.issue: `is_null( $order->get_meta() )` is used as an early return guard. `WC_Order::get_meta()` without arguments returns a single meta value for an empty key, not all meta data — the correct early return should check `$order->get_meta_data()` instead.
code_quality.fix: Changed the early return guard to `if ( empty( $order->get_meta_data() ) ) { return; }` to correctly short-circuit on orders with no meta.

dependency_integrity.issue: Depends on `_get_meta_table()`, `LegacyProxy::class`, `wc_get_container()`, and `$this->internal_meta_keys` — all WooCommerce internals expected to be present in context.

i18n.issue: The logger warning string is an internal developer-facing diagnostic, not a user-facing string, so translation is not required.

accessibility.issue: `update_order_meta_from_object` is a database persistence method with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply.

critique.key_observation: The logger is re-instantiated via `wc_get_container()->get( LegacyProxy::class )->call_function( 'wc_get_logger' )` on every iteration of the inner loop, and the early return guard `is_null( $order->get_meta() )` tests the wrong method — it should be `get_meta_data()`.

critique.summary: `print_r( $request->get_params(), true )` dumps all request parameters — potentially including payment tokens, card details, or auth credentials — into an error log in the catch block, and the `sprintf` format string has only two `%s` placeholders in the translated string but three arguments are passed.

wpcs_compliance.issue: `print_r()` is a development function prohibited in production code by WPCS. The `esc_html__()` translatable string `'Error completing order cancel with %1$s. %2$s %3$s'` uses three numbered placeholders but the catch block comment only mentions two translators — the third `%3$s` placeholder substitutes `print_r()` output which is debug data, not a translatable token.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Removed `print_r( $request->get_params(), true )` from the sprintf arguments and from the format string. Updated the translatable string to use only two placeholders.

sql_safety.issue: `process_async_cancel` delegates all persistence to `$order->update_status()` and WC logger — no raw SQL queries.

security.issue: `print_r( $request->get_params(), true )` logs all request parameters to the WooCommerce error log. In a payment gateway cancel callback, request parameters may include transaction IDs, tokens, customer data, or credentials that should not be stored in log files (PCI-DSS consideration).
security.fix: Removed `print_r( $request->get_params(), true )` from the log message. If request context is needed for debugging, log only the specific safe parameters such as the transaction ID.

performance.issue: Single request handling with redirect — no performance concern.

wp_api_usage.issue: `esc_html__()` is used to produce a string that is then passed to `$order->update_status()` as an order note. Order notes accept HTML, so `esc_html__()` is overly restrictive here — `__()` with late escaping at display time would be more appropriate. Also, `wc_price()` returns HTML markup which will be HTML-escaped by `esc_html__()` — this is an escaping mismatch.
wp_api_usage.fix: Changed `esc_html__()` to `__()` for the order note text to preserve `wc_price()` HTML markup. Applied `wp_kses_post()` when the note is rendered, not when built.

code_quality.issue: The sprintf format string `'Error completing order cancel with %1$s. %2$s %3$s'` has three numbered placeholders but three arguments are passed: `$this->id`, `$e->getMessage()`, and `print_r(...)`. After removing the print_r argument, the format string must be updated to only reference `%1$s` and `%2$s`.
code_quality.fix: Updated format string to `'Error completing order cancel with %1$s. %2$s'` and removed the third argument.

dependency_integrity.issue: Depends on `Utils::get_request()`, `$this->validate_request()`, `$this->gateway`, and `$this->get_order()` — all expected class methods. No external dependency issues.

i18n.issue: The translatable string for the note text wraps `wc_price( $order->get_total() )` (which returns HTML) in `esc_html__()`, causing the HTML price tag to be displayed as escaped literal text in the order note rather than rendered markup.
i18n.fix: Changed to use `__()` instead of `esc_html__()` for the note text so wc_price() output is preserved as HTML in the order timeline.

accessibility.issue: `process_async_cancel` handles a payment gateway callback and redirect — no HTML UI is rendered by this method directly.

critique.key_observation: `print_r( $request->get_params(), true )` logged in the catch block exposes all incoming payment gateway request parameters — potentially including tokens and credentials — to the WooCommerce error log.

critique.summary: The function body is truncated mid-query-string after `"SELECT COUNT( DISTINCT {$wpdb->posts}`, making it syntactically invalid PHP; additionally the critical_failures flag identifies a potential N+1 database query pattern inside a loop.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Function body is truncated — missing the closing quote, closing array bracket, the full query construction, and the `$wpdb->get_col()` call. The incomplete code will produce a PHP parse error. The `$query_type` parameter default `'and'` is lowercase but the comparison in the outer code uses `'or'` — the logic is consistent but `'and'` is never explicitly checked.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Completed the query construction and execution. Added `absint()` for each `$term_id` in the results loop.

sql_safety.issue: The truncated `SELECT COUNT( DISTINCT {$wpdb->posts}` string suggests the full query will interpolate `$wpdb->posts` (safe) and additional JOINs from `$meta_query_sql` and `$tax_query_sql`. These SQL fragments are generated by WP core's `WP_Meta_Query::get_sql()` and `WP_Tax_Query::get_sql()` which are internally parameterized, but the `$term_ids` array injected into the IN clause must use `$wpdb->prepare()` with `%d` placeholders.
sql_safety.fix: Completed the query with `implode( ',', array_fill( 0, count($term_ids), '%d' ) )` for the term_id IN clause, passed through `$wpdb->prepare()`.

security.issue: `$term_ids` is an array passed in from the widget — if not validated as integer IDs, these could contain non-integer values injected into the SQL term_ids IN clause.
security.fix: Applied `array_map( 'absint', $term_ids )` to ensure all term IDs are positive integers before building the query.

performance.issue: The critical_failures flag identifies a potential N+1 pattern — the full WooCommerce implementation of this method executes `$wpdb->get_col()` once per widget render for each taxonomy filter. The intended implementation aggregates all term counts in a single query using GROUP BY `term_id`, which is the correct approach. The incomplete code suggests the single-query pattern is intended but not verified due to truncation.
performance.fix: Completed the query to use a single `GROUP BY` aggregation rather than per-term queries, returning a map of `term_id => count`.

wp_api_usage.issue: `WP_Meta_Query::get_sql()` and `WP_Tax_Query::get_sql()` are used correctly to build filter-aware SQL fragments. No WP API misuse.
wp_api_usage.fix: No wp_api_usage changes needed.

code_quality.issue: The function is truncated mid-string-literal — the code as written will cause a PHP parse error. The complete implementation is missing.
code_quality.fix: Completed the function with the missing SELECT columns, FROM clause, JOINs, WHERE, GROUP BY, and `$wpdb->get_col()` execution.

dependency_integrity.issue: Depends on `WC_Query::get_main_tax_query()`, `WC_Query::get_main_meta_query()`, `WP_Meta_Query`, and `WP_Tax_Query` — all WooCommerce and WP core classes expected to be available.

i18n.issue: `get_filtered_term_product_counts` is a data-fetching method returning a count array — no translatable strings.

accessibility.issue: `get_filtered_term_product_counts` returns an array of counts — no HTML output, no accessibility concerns.

critique.key_observation: The function is truncated mid-SQL-string making it syntactically invalid; the complete implementation must use `$wpdb->prepare()` with `%d` placeholders for the `$term_ids` IN clause.

critique.summary: `esc_html__()` is applied to the entire format string and its HTML anchor tag placeholders are then injected via `sprintf()`, producing an inconsistent escaping pattern where the translated string is HTML-escaped but raw HTML is injected via the substitution arguments; additionally `$this->shortlinker->get()` output is not passed through `esc_url()`.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Fully-qualified function calls `\sprintf` and `\esc_html__` use a leading backslash which is unnecessary in global namespace PHP and reduces readability. WordPress Coding Standards prefers unqualified calls in plugin/theme code.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Removed leading backslashes from `\sprintf` and `\esc_html__`.

sql_safety.issue: `get_success_description` performs no database operations.

security.issue: `$this->shortlinker->get( 'https://yoa.st/3zw' )` is interpolated directly into an `href` attribute without `esc_url()`. If `shortlinker->get()` returns an attacker-influenced URL (e.g., a `javascript:` scheme), this creates an XSS vector. Additionally, `esc_html__()` wrapping the sprintf format string HTML-escapes the placeholder markers — the pattern is structurally broken because the placeholders `%1$s` and `%2$s` receive raw HTML `<a>` tags as substitution arguments.
security.fix: Wrapped `$this->shortlinker->get()` with `esc_url()`. Changed the output pattern to use `wp_kses_post()` on the final assembled string, or separated the text portions from the HTML.

performance.issue: `get_success_description` is a simple string construction method — no performance concern.

wp_api_usage.issue: Using `esc_html__()` as the format string argument to `sprintf()` when the substituted values are HTML anchor tags is incorrect — `esc_html` encodes `<`, `>`, `&`, `"` which would prevent the `<a>` tags from rendering as links. The correct pattern is to use `__()` for the translatable format string and then apply `wp_kses_post()` to the final assembled output.
wp_api_usage.fix: Changed `esc_html__()` to `__()` for the format string. Applied `wp_kses_post()` to the complete `sprintf()` result. Applied `esc_url()` to the shortlinker URL.

code_quality.issue: Mixing HTML injection via `sprintf()` placeholders with `esc_html__()` on the format string creates a pattern that is both semantically incorrect and misleading — the escaping function suggests safety but the HTML injected via substitution bypasses it. The pattern must be restructured.
code_quality.fix: Restructured to use `__()` for the translatable string and `wp_kses_post()` on the assembled output so both translation and HTML injection work correctly.

dependency_integrity.issue: Depends on `$this->shortlinker` and `WPSEO_Admin_Utils::get_new_tab_message()` — expected Yoast SEO internal dependencies.

i18n.issue: The translatable string `'%1$s Find out how the text link counter can enhance your SEO%2$s.'` includes HTML anchor tags as placeholders, which is acceptable for i18n (translators get context via the comment). However, applying `esc_html__()` to the format string will HTML-encode the `<` and `>` in the substitution arguments, breaking the link rendering. The correct pattern uses `__()` with a translators comment.
i18n.fix: Changed `esc_html__()` to `__()` and verified the translators comment accurately describes the placeholder content.

accessibility.issue: The anchor tag opens with `target="_blank"` but the new-tab icon from `WPSEO_Admin_Utils::get_new_tab_message()` must include a screen-reader-only text indicating the link opens in a new tab. If `get_new_tab_message()` does not provide this, the link behavior is invisible to screen reader users.
accessibility.fix: Noted that `WPSEO_Admin_Utils::get_new_tab_message()` is expected to provide the screen-reader text for the new tab behavior — no change needed at this call site if that method functions correctly.

critique.key_observation: `esc_html__()` applied to a format string that receives `<a>` tag HTML via `sprintf()` is a broken escaping pattern — `esc_html` encodes the angle brackets in the substituted anchor tags, preventing them from rendering as links while creating a false impression of XSS safety.

unaddressed_issues.fix_text: Changed `esc_html__()` to `__()` and verified the translators comment accurately describes the placeholder content.

critique.summary: `$_GET['use-ppc']` is accessed without sanitization — while the strict `=== 'true'` comparison prevents value injection, the unsanitized superglobal access on the right side of `&&` is evaluated after `! empty()` passes, making the pattern fragile and violating WPCS sanitization requirements.

wpcs_compliance.issue: `$_GET['use-ppc']` is accessed without `wp_unslash()` and `sanitize_text_field()` (or `sanitize_key()`). The PHPCS suppression comment silences the nonce warning but does not address the missing sanitization. WPCS requires all superglobal access to be sanitized regardless of how the value is used.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Applied `isset()` check and `sanitize_key( wp_unslash( $_GET['use-ppc'] ) )` before comparison.

sql_safety.issue: `wc_gateway_ppec_is_using_credit` performs no database queries.

security.issue: `$_GET['use-ppc']` is read without sanitization. Although the `=== 'true'` strict comparison means only the exact string `'true'` returns true, the right-hand operand of `&&` still accesses the raw superglobal. WPCS suppression comments do not fix the underlying issue — the value should be sanitized before use.
security.fix: Added `isset( $_GET['use-ppc'] )` check and applied `sanitize_key( wp_unslash( $_GET['use-ppc'] ) )` so the sanitized value is compared to `'true'`.

performance.issue: Single superglobal access with no computation — no performance concern.

wp_api_usage.issue: The phpcs:ignore suppression for `WordPress.Security.NonceVerification.Recommended` is appropriate here because this is a query-string flag read during checkout flow where nonce verification would break the PayPal redirect flow. However, `wp_unslash()` and `sanitize_key()` should still be applied.
wp_api_usage.fix: Added `wp_unslash()` and `sanitize_key()` wrapping the `$_GET` access, retaining the phpcs:ignore for nonce verification.

code_quality.issue: The double access of `$_GET['use-ppc']` — once in `! empty()` and once in `=== 'true'` — can be simplified to a single `isset()` + sanitized comparison.
code_quality.fix: Refactored to a single `isset()` + sanitize + compare expression.

dependency_integrity.issue: `wc_gateway_ppec_is_using_credit` has no external dependencies beyond WordPress superglobals.

i18n.issue: Function returns a boolean — no translatable strings.

accessibility.issue: `wc_gateway_ppec_is_using_credit` is a boolean utility function with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply.

critique.key_observation: `$_GET['use-ppc']` is accessed twice without sanitization, relying on a PHPCS suppression comment rather than applying `wp_unslash()` and `sanitize_key()` which are required regardless of nonce verification status.

critique.summary: `wp_cache_disable` returns `true` when the cache is already disabled but returns `null` (no return statement) on successful disable, making it impossible for callers to reliably determine whether the disable operation succeeded; and the function lacks a PHPDoc block and a capability check.

wpcs_compliance.issue: No PHPDoc block is present — missing `@return bool`, `@since`, and description. The inconsistent return values (`true` on one path, implicit `null` on the other) violate the documented return type contract that callers expect.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Added PHPDoc block with `@return bool` and `@since`. Added an explicit `return true` after the successful disable path.

sql_safety.issue: `wp_cache_disable` modifies a config file and clears scheduled hooks — no SQL queries.

security.issue: No `current_user_can()` check guards `wp_cache_disable`. Any code path that reaches this function — including malicious plugins or CSRF-triggered admin actions — can disable the cache without permission verification.
security.fix: Added `current_user_can( 'manage_options' )` check at the top of the function, returning false if the current user lacks the required capability.

performance.issue: Three `wp_clear_scheduled_hook()` calls are made sequentially — each performs a DB lookup and delete. This is the standard pattern and no optimization is needed for an admin-triggered operation.

wp_api_usage.issue: `wp_clear_scheduled_hook()` and `wp_cache_setting()` are the correct APIs for this operation. `wp_cache_debug()` is an internal WP Super Cache function used appropriately for debug logging.
wp_api_usage.fix: No wp_api_usage changes needed.

code_quality.issue: The function returns `true` when cache is already disabled (early return) but has no return statement when the cache is successfully disabled, returning `null`. Callers checking `if ( wp_cache_disable() )` will get `true` when nothing changed and `null` (falsy) when disable succeeded — the opposite of what is expected.
code_quality.fix: Added `return true` after the scheduled hook clearing to normalize the return value to `bool` on both code paths.

dependency_integrity.issue: Depends on `wp_cache_setting()`, `wp_cache_debug()`, and `wp_clear_scheduled_hook()`. The latter is a WP core function always available; the former two are WP Super Cache internals expected to be loaded.

i18n.issue: `wp_cache_debug()` messages are developer-facing diagnostic strings — no translation needed.

accessibility.issue: `wp_cache_disable` is a cache management utility with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply.

critique.key_observation: The inconsistent return value — `true` on the early-return path and implicit `null` on the success path — inverts the expected boolean semantics for callers checking whether the disable operation succeeded.

critique.summary: `$this->sql` and `$this->count_sql` are passed directly to `$wpdb->get_col()` and `$wpdb->get_var()` with `phpcs:ignore WordPress.DB.PreparedSQL.NotPrepared` comments, indicating the caller suppressed the warning rather than fixing it; the SQL properties must be verified to be prepared before execution.

wpcs_compliance.issue: `phpcs:ignore WordPress.DB.PreparedSQL.NotPrepared` is used twice to suppress SQL preparation warnings rather than addressing the underlying issue. WPCS requires SQL queries passed to `$wpdb` methods to be prepared. Suppression is only acceptable when the SQL is constructed entirely from safe, sanitized components.
wpcs_compliance.fix: If `$this->sql` and `$this->count_sql` are assembled from `$wpdb->prepare()` calls elsewhere in the class, the ignore comments are acceptable. Added documentation to the docblock clarifying that `$this->sql` must be a pre-prepared query string. Applied `$this->sql` guard to verify it is a non-empty string before executing.

sql_safety.issue: `$this->sql` is passed directly to `$wpdb->get_col()` without any verification that it was prepared. The `phpcs:ignore` comment bypasses the PHPCS check but does not guarantee the SQL is safe. If `$this->sql` is ever assigned from user-influenced input without `$wpdb->prepare()`, this is a direct SQL injection point.
sql_safety.fix: Added an `if ( empty( $this->sql ) ) { return; }` guard. Documented that `$this->sql` must be constructed exclusively via `$wpdb->prepare()` in the query-building methods of the parent class. Since `run_query()` cannot call `$wpdb->prepare()` without knowing the dynamic values (those are assembled in the builder), retained the ignore comment with explicit documentation.

security.issue: If `$this->args['limit']` is zero or unset, the division `$this->found_orders / $this->args['limit']` will produce a division-by-zero error or PHP warning.
security.fix: Added a guard: `$limit = ! empty( $this->args['limit'] ) ? absint( $this->args['limit'] ) : 1;` before the `ceil()` calculation.

performance.issue: Two queries are executed per `run_query()` call when limits are active: one for results and one for the total count. This is the standard WP_Query pagination pattern and is acceptable.

wp_api_usage.issue: `$wpdb->get_col()` and `$wpdb->get_var()` are the correct methods for fetching a column of IDs and a single integer respectively. `array_map( 'absint', ... )` correctly sanitizes the returned order IDs.
wp_api_usage.fix: No wp_api_usage changes needed.

code_quality.issue: The docblock says `@return void` but the function returns early at two points — this is consistent with `void` return type but the conditions for early return are not documented. The division `$this->found_orders / $this->args['limit']` is not guarded against division by zero.
code_quality.fix: Added guard for `$this->args['limit']` being zero. Added inline comment explaining the early return conditions.

dependency_integrity.issue: Depends on `$this->sql`, `$this->count_sql`, `$this->limits`, `$this->args`, `$this->orders`, `$this->found_orders`, `$this->max_num_pages` — all expected instance properties of the WC_Order_Query class.

i18n.issue: `run_query` performs database retrieval with no user-facing output — no i18n concerns.

accessibility.issue: `run_query` is a data-fetching method with no HTML output — accessibility concerns do not apply.

critique.key_observation: `$this->sql` is executed via `$wpdb->get_col()` with a PHPCS suppression comment rather than a verified `$wpdb->prepare()` call — the safety of this query entirely depends on the query-builder methods in the parent class constructing `$this->sql` via `prepare()`, which is not enforced or documented at the execution site.

critique.summary: The function `get_wpdb()` is declared as a bare standalone function despite `Wrapper::get_wpdb` context implying it is a class method; the PHPDoc is also missing the mandatory `@since` tag required by WordPress Coding Standards for public API methods.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Function `get_wpdb` lacks a `@since` tag in its PHPDoc block, violating WordPress Coding Standards which require `@since` on all public API methods. Indentation uses double-tabs inconsistent with WPCS single-tab standard.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Add `@since` tag (e.g. `@since 1.0.0`) to the PHPDoc block and normalize indentation to single tabs throughout `get_wpdb`.

sql_safety.issue: Function `get_wpdb` only returns the `$wpdb` global; it does not build or execute any queries, so no SQL safety concern exists at this surface.
sql_safety.fix: No SQL remediation needed in `get_wpdb`; callers are responsible for using `$wpdb->prepare()` on any queries they construct with the returned object.

security.issue: Function `get_wpdb` simply exposes the `$wpdb` global for dependency injection; it introduces no new attack surface beyond what `global $wpdb` already provides to callers.
security.fix: No security remediation needed in `get_wpdb` itself; the dependency-injection pattern is correct and safer than having every consumer declare `global $wpdb` directly.

performance.issue: Function `get_wpdb` returns a single global reference; there is no performance concern.

wp_api_usage.issue: Function `get_wpdb` correctly accesses the `$wpdb` global; no WP API misuse exists here.
wp_api_usage.fix: No WP API remediation needed for `get_wpdb`.

code_quality.issue: The `function_name` field is `Wrapper::get_wpdb` indicating a class method, but the code is a bare `function get_wpdb()` with no class declaration. The excessive leading indentation (two tabs) suggests it was extracted incorrectly from a class body, leaving it as an orphaned standalone function that cannot use `$this` or benefit from class scope.
code_quality.fix: Wrap `get_wpdb` in its proper class declaration (`class Wrapper { ... }`) so that it is a genuine instance method. If it must remain standalone, remove the class prefix from the name and normalize to zero leading indentation.

dependency_integrity.issue: Function `get_wpdb` depends only on the `$wpdb` global, which is always available after WordPress loads.

i18n.issue: Function `get_wpdb` outputs no strings; no i18n concern applies.

accessibility.issue: Function `get_wpdb` produces no HTML output; accessibility concerns do not apply.
accessibility.fix: No accessibility remediation needed; add ARIA attributes in any view layer that consumes query results from `get_wpdb`.

critique.key_observation: The core defect is structural: `get_wpdb` is presented as a class method `Wrapper::get_wpdb` but the code is a bare function with orphaned indentation, making it uninstantiable and inconsistent with its documented purpose.

critique.summary: Function `search_by_title_only` is truncated mid-string-concatenation, leaving the `$search` SQL fragment unclosed and the function body without a closing brace; additionally, `esc_sql()` wrapping `$wpdb->esc_like()` is the correct WP pattern but the visible LIKE clause embeds the result via interpolation without `$wpdb->prepare()`.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Function `search_by_title_only` has a double-tab leading indentation throughout the body, inconsistent with WPCS single-tab standard, and the `@param` type hint for `WP_Query` should include the FQCN format `\WP_Query`.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Normalize body indentation of `search_by_title_only` to single tabs and update the `@param` annotation to `@param \WP_Query $wp_query`.

sql_safety.issue: In `search_by_title_only`, the LIKE fragment is built by string interpolation (`"{$searchand}($wpdb->posts.post_title LIKE '{$n}...'`) without `$wpdb->prepare()`. Even though `esc_sql($wpdb->esc_like($term))` is used for the term itself, the overall query clause is concatenated unsafely and the function is truncated so the interpolated term is never actually escaped in the visible code.
sql_safety.fix: Complete the LIKE clause using `$wpdb->prepare()` with a `%s` placeholder for the escaped-and-liked term: `$wpdb->prepare( "($wpdb->posts.post_title LIKE %s)", $n . $wpdb->esc_like( $term ) . $n )`.

security.issue: Function `search_by_title_only` accepts `$search` from `WP_Query` internals which ultimately originates from user-supplied GET/POST input; the truncated code leaves an open SQL string that could be injected into if the function were to execute as-is.
security.fix: Use `$wpdb->prepare()` for the full LIKE expression as described in sql_safety; do not rely solely on `esc_sql`+`esc_like` interpolation.

performance.issue: Function `search_by_title_only` builds SQL inside a `foreach` loop over `$q['search_terms']`, meaning one LIKE term is appended per iteration. While this is a filter on the query rather than a separate DB call, iterating without a count limit allows unbounded search terms to bloat the WHERE clause.
performance.fix: Add a guard like `$search_terms = array_slice( (array) $q['search_terms'], 0, 10 );` before the `foreach` in `search_by_title_only` to cap the number of LIKE clauses.

wp_api_usage.issue: Function `search_by_title_only` is correctly wired as a `posts_search` filter and uses `$wpdb->esc_like()` appropriately; no WP API misuse is present beyond the missing `prepare()` call.
wp_api_usage.fix: No additional WP API changes needed beyond the `$wpdb->prepare()` fix already described.

code_quality.issue: Function `search_by_title_only` is truncated mid-statement — the `$search .=` assignment is an unclosed string with no closing quote, paren, semicolon, or closing brace, making the function syntactically invalid PHP.
code_quality.fix: Complete the foreach body with the correct LIKE clause, the closing `$searchand = ' AND ';` update, the `$search` prefix/suffix wrapping, and the function's closing brace.

dependency_integrity.issue: Function `search_by_title_only` depends only on the `$wpdb` global and `$wp_query->query_vars`, both of which are guaranteed available in the `posts_search` filter context.

i18n.issue: Function `search_by_title_only` generates SQL clauses, not user-visible strings; no i18n concern applies.

accessibility.issue: Function `search_by_title_only` produces no HTML output; accessibility concerns do not apply.
accessibility.fix: No accessibility remediation needed for `search_by_title_only`; ARIA roles belong in the search results template, not this query filter.

critique.key_observation: The function is syntactically incomplete — the LIKE clause is an unclosed string mid-interpolation — making it undeployable as-is; the critical fix is completing the function with proper `$wpdb->prepare()` parameterization.

critique.summary: Function `get_stylesheet` uses `file_get_contents()` on a local path on every call with no caching, and the function body is truncated — missing the closing `return $css;` and closing brace.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` has a correctly formed PHPDoc block; the phpcs:ignore comment is present and appropriate. Only minor: body indentation uses double-tabs instead of the WPCS single-tab standard.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Normalize `get_stylesheet` body indentation to single tabs to comply with WPCS.

sql_safety.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` performs no database operations; SQL safety does not apply.

security.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` reads from `$this->use_stylesheet` or `plvt_get_asset_path(...)`. If `$this->use_stylesheet` is ever set from user-controlled input, passing it to `file_get_contents()` creates a local-file-read risk. The path should be validated to stay within the plugin directory.
security.fix: Add `realpath()` validation in `get_stylesheet` to confirm the resolved path starts with the plugin base directory before calling `file_get_contents()`, e.g.: `if ( strpos( realpath( $stylesheet_path ), realpath( plugin_dir_path( __FILE__ ) ) ) !== 0 ) { return ''; }`.

performance.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` calls `file_get_contents()` on every invocation with no static variable or transient cache, causing repeated filesystem reads for the same CSS file on every page request that triggers this animation.
performance.fix: Introduce a static cache in `get_stylesheet`: `static $cache = []; $cache_key = $this->slug . $alias; if ( isset( $cache[$cache_key] ) ) { return $cache[$cache_key]; }` and set `$cache[$cache_key] = $css;` before returning.

wp_api_usage.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` uses `file_get_contents()` instead of the WordPress Filesystem API (`WP_Filesystem` / `$wp_filesystem->get_contents()`), which is the WordPress-idiomatic way to read local files and is required in contexts where filesystem abstraction matters.
wp_api_usage.fix: Replace `file_get_contents( $stylesheet_path )` in `get_stylesheet` with WP Filesystem: call `global $wp_filesystem; WP_Filesystem(); $css = $wp_filesystem->get_contents( $stylesheet_path );`.

code_quality.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` is truncated — the `if ( (bool) $this->use_stylesheet )` block has no closing brace, and the function has no `return $css;` statement or function-closing brace, making this syntactically invalid PHP.
code_quality.fix: Complete `get_stylesheet` by closing the `if` block, adding `return $css;`, and closing the function brace.

dependency_integrity.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` depends on `plvt_get_asset_path()`, a plugin-defined helper; as long as the plugin loads correctly this dependency is satisfied.
dependency_integrity.fix: No dependency remediation needed for `get_stylesheet`; optionally add `function_exists( 'plvt_get_asset_path' )` guard for defensive coding.

i18n.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` returns raw CSS content with no user-visible strings; no i18n concern applies.

accessibility.issue: Function `get_stylesheet` returns a CSS string; it has no HTML surface and accessibility concerns do not apply at this level.
accessibility.fix: No accessibility remediation needed for `get_stylesheet`; animation CSS should be designed to respect `prefers-reduced-motion` in the CSS itself.

critique.key_observation: The function is syntactically incomplete (missing closing brace and return), and the performance defect of uncached repeated `file_get_contents()` calls on every request is the most impactful runtime issue.

critique.summary: Function `html_section_item_actions` outputs `<button>` elements with data attributes sourced from `$type` and `$item_label` without escaping, creating an XSS vector, and the button text is not wrapped in `<label>` or `aria-label` context for screen readers.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Function `html_section_item_actions` uses short array syntax `[]` where WPCS prefers `array()`, and the PHPDoc block is missing entirely for a public method.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Add a PHPDoc block with `@since`, `@return string`, and convert `[]` to `array()` in `html_section_item_actions`.

sql_safety.issue: Function `html_section_item_actions` makes no database queries; SQL safety does not apply.

security.issue: In `html_section_item_actions`, `$type` from `CourseModel::item_types_support()` and `$item_label` from `CourseModel::item_types_label()` are interpolated directly into HTML via `sprintf` with `%s` without escaping. If these values contain `<`, `>`, or `"` characters, they produce XSS.
security.fix: Wrap all dynamic values in `esc_attr()` for HTML attribute context and `esc_html()` for text node context in `html_section_item_actions`: use `esc_attr( $type )` for `data-item-type`, and `esc_attr( sprintf(...) )` for the data-placeholder/data-button-add-text attributes, and `esc_html( sprintf(...) )` for the button label.

performance.issue: Function `html_section_item_actions` calls `CourseModel::item_types_label()` once per iteration; if this method is expensive, it could be memoized, but without seeing its implementation this is speculative.
performance.fix: No performance remediation needed for `html_section_item_actions` without evidence that `item_types_label` is expensive.

wp_api_usage.issue: Function `html_section_item_actions` uses `__()` correctly with the `'learnpress'` text domain; no WP API misuse present.
wp_api_usage.fix: No WP API remediation needed for `html_section_item_actions`.

code_quality.issue: Function `html_section_item_actions` uses short array `[]` syntax inconsistently alongside `array()` usage in the same codebase; and the missing PHPDoc reduces discoverability.
code_quality.fix: Standardize to `array()` throughout `html_section_item_actions` and add a PHPDoc block.

dependency_integrity.issue: Function `html_section_item_actions` depends on `CourseModel` and `Template` classes; these are plugin-internal dependencies that should always be available in the LearnPress context.

i18n.issue: Function `html_section_item_actions` correctly uses `__()` with the `'learnpress'` text domain for all user-visible strings; i18n is handled properly.

accessibility.issue: The `<button>` elements in `html_section_item_actions` have visible text content ('New %s') but the `data-placeholder` and `data-button-add-text` attributes hold translated strings used by JavaScript — if JS replaces the button text dynamically, the accessible name may become empty. Additionally, no `aria-label` is provided as a fallback.
accessibility.fix: Add `aria-label` attributes to each button in `html_section_item_actions` with the fully composed translated string, e.g. `aria-label="%s"` passed `esc_attr( sprintf( __( 'Add new %s to course', 'learnpress' ), $item_label ) )` so screen readers always have a meaningful label regardless of JS state.

critique.key_observation: The critical defect is unescaped output of `$type` and `$item_label` into HTML attributes and text nodes via `sprintf`, creating a stored XSS vector if those values are ever attacker-influenced.

critique.summary: This is a PHPUnit REST API test method in the Gutenberg test suite that verifies EXIF metadata extraction; it is test code with no production security, SQL, or accessibility surface.

wpcs_compliance.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` uses double-tab indentation; WPCS requires single-tab. The PHPDoc already contains proper `@covers` and `@requires` annotations.
wpcs_compliance.fix: Normalize indentation in `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` to single tabs.

sql_safety.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` makes no direct database queries; SQL safety does not apply here.
sql_safety.fix: No SQL remediation needed for this test method.

security.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` operates in a controlled PHPUnit test environment with a fixed test image file; no production security surface exists.
security.fix: No security remediation needed for this test method.

performance.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` calls `file_get_contents()` on a test fixture, which is acceptable in a test context. No performance concern for unit tests.
performance.fix: No performance remediation needed for this test method.

wp_api_usage.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` correctly uses `WP_REST_Request`, `rest_get_server()->dispatch()`, and `wp_set_current_user()` — all appropriate WP test APIs.
wp_api_usage.fix: No WP API remediation needed for this test method.

code_quality.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` is well-structured with clear arrange/act/assert phases and good inline comments explaining the test fixture's properties.
code_quality.fix: No code quality changes needed; the test is clear and well-annotated.

dependency_integrity.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` depends on `DIR_TESTDATA` being defined and the test image existing; both are guaranteed by the WordPress test bootstrap.
dependency_integrity.fix: No dependency remediation needed; `@requires extension exif` correctly gates the test.

i18n.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` contains no user-facing strings requiring translation.
i18n.fix: No i18n remediation needed for this test method.

accessibility.issue: Test method `test_full_exif_metadata_extracted_for_client_side_upload` produces no HTML output; accessibility concerns do not apply.
accessibility.fix: No accessibility remediation needed for this test method.
